solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing checks for exact literal strings 'dayjs(item.x[1]).valueOf()' and 'dayjs(item.x[0]).valueOf()' via substring matching. An alternative correct fix like 'dayjs(item.x[1]).unix() * 1000' or 'Number(dayjs(item.x[1]))' or '+dayjs(item.x[1])' would fail despite being functionally correct.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing is purely text-matching ('dayjs(item.x[1]).valueOf()' in content). While other tests (test_vitest_unit_tests_pass, test_typescript_compiles, test_repo_build, etc.) do execute subprocesses, and test_date_parsing_produces_valid_numbers/test_chart_options_scale_calculation run Node scripts, the primary fail-to-pass test (test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing) is entirely string-in-file checking with no execution.

test_not_tautological.reason: test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing can be satisfied by simply doing a string replacement in the file without understanding the bug. test_date_parsing_produces_valid_numbers and test_chart_options_scale_calculation run independent Node scripts that test dayjs behavior in isolation — they don't actually import or run the modified utils.ts code, so they pass regardless of the actual file content (they test dayjs itself, not the fix).

anti_cheating_measures.reason: The solution is available at /workspace/airflow in the container (solve.sh contains the exact patch). The agent could read solve.sh or the git diff. Also, the test_dayjs_used_for_date_parsing test checks for exact literal strings that are stated verbatim in instruction.md, so the agent could just do a sed replacement matching those strings without understanding anything.
